content: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of the CNS characterized by inflammation, demyelination, and neurodegeneration. Clinical courses: relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS, 85% at onset) with discrete attacks followed by remission; secondary progressive MS (SPMS) with gradual worsening after initial RRMS; primary progressive MS (PPMS, 15%) with steady progression from onset. Pathophysiology involves T-cell mediated attack on myelin oligodendrocytes, blood-brain barrier disruption, and formation of periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord plaques. McDonald criteria (2017 revision) require dissemination in space (≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 characteristic CNS regions) and time (simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or new T2/enhancing lesion on follow-up). CSF oligoclonal bands support diagnosis. Progressive disease involves microglial activation and compartmentalized inflammation behind closed blood-brain barrier.
